steps.title: Find Text Two's criticism
steps.content: Text 1 presents the traditional view that birdsong is a male trait used to attract mates and claim territory. Text 2 says that view is a historical mischaracterization. Mendez and colleagues argue that the error came largely from research bias: studies were often done near universities in the temperate northern hemisphere, where female birdsong is less common than in the tropics.

steps.title: Reject wrong responses
steps.content: Choice A concerns what drove the evolution of singing in male birds, but Text 2 does not replace mate attraction and territorial defense with other male-song causes. Choice B compares the purposes of male birdsong in temperate and tropical regions, which Text 2 never does. Choice D blames a tendency to study male birds, but Text 2 blames the places researchers tended to study.

steps.title: Tie bias to study sites
steps.content: Mendez and colleagues would respond that the old male-trait view was shaped by geographic sampling: researchers selected temperate study sites near universities, where female birdsong is relatively uncommon. That explains why researchers overlooked female birdsong across species. The answer is C.
